narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 31, lower than the usual 36.70. The admissions model predicted an 80.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, a risk level that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and significantly pushed the model toward forecasting bar failure, since stronger late-stage academic performance is a key indicator of bar success. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 1, again at the lowest level compared to the average around 5 or 6, reinforcing the model's concern about your early academic struggles carrying forward. Your undergraduate GPA offered some counterbalance, as it nudged the prediction slightly toward passing despite being below average, showing that your college record wasn't a complete drag. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles throughout school, your consistently low rankings in both early and later years set you apart as higher risk, even with your full-time commitment aligning with the vast majority of successful applicants. These academic decile factors were the top drivers, outweighing positives like your family income bracket. While this is disappointing, especially given your dedication to full-time study, it highlights how foundational grades in law school matter immensely for bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that could reshape your profile significantly. Strengthening your academic foundation through targeted prep courses or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward momentum before reapplying. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on, and with your background, you're well-positioned to improve. Reapplying after building a stronger record could turn this into just a temporary setback.
